file_name: Apollo Endosurgery - Manufacturing and Supply Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: APOLLO may inspect any or all shipments of Product to insure all specifications are met including proper labeling, packaging and count within thirty (30) business days of APOLLO's receipt of each shipment; however, any such inspection shall not relieve ESTABLISHMENT of any obligations or warranties under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: APOLLO may inspect any or all shipments of Product to insure all specifications are met including proper labeling, packaging and count within ten (10) business days of APOLLO's receipt of each shipment; however, any such inspection shall not relieve ESTABLISHMENT of any obligations or warranties under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the inspection period to 10 days may be insufficient under the UCC and general commercial reasonableness standards, potentially conflicting with a buyer's right to a reasonable inspection period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCC § 2-606. What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-606 dictates that acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer has had a reasonable opportunity to inspect them. Reducing the inspection window to ten days may not be considered a reasonable opportunity to inspect, especially for complex medical devices, thus potentially conflicting with the UCC's provisions regarding acceptance and rejection of goods.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-606. What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-606. What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods. (1) Acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer (a) after a reasonable opportunity to inspect the goods signifies to the seller that the goods are conforming or that he will take or retain them in spite of their non-conformity; or (b) fails to make an effective rejection (subsection (1) of Section 2-602 ), but such acceptance does not occur until the buyer has had a reasonable opportunity to inspect them; or (c) does any act inconsistent with the seller's ownership; but if such act is wrongful as against the seller it is an acceptance only if ratified by him. (2) Acceptance of a part of any commercial unit is acceptance of that entire unit. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the inspection period from thirty business days to ten business days. UCC § 2-606 states that acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer has had a reasonable opportunity to inspect them. By reducing the inspection period, the buyer may not have a 'reasonable opportunity to inspect the goods' as required by law, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party's agreement to indemnify, defend and hold the other harmless is conditioned on the indemnified Party (i) providing written notice to the indemnifying Party of any claim, demand or action arising out of the indemnified activities within thirty (30) days after the indemnified Party has knowledge of -10- such claim, demand or action
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party's agreement to indemnify, defend and hold the other harmless is conditioned on the indemnified Party (i) providing written notice to the indemnifying Party of any claim, demand or action arising out of the indemnified activities within five (5) days after the indemnified Party has knowledge of -10- such claim, demand or action
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for indemnification claims to 5 days may be considered commercially unreasonable and could conflict with statutes of limitations or legal requirements for timely notification of claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 16.070. Agreement to Shorten Limitations Period
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 16.070 allows parties to agree to shorten a statute of limitations, but it must be reasonable. Reducing the notification period for indemnification to 5 days might be deemed unreasonably short, especially given the complexities of discovering and assessing potential liabilities. This could render the indemnification clause unenforceable under Texas law.

(c) Service of a notice under Subsection (b) must be by first class or certified mail and is complete when the notice is deposited in the United States mail, postage prepaid and addressed to the debtor at the debtor's last known address. The affidavit of a person knowledgeable of the facts to the effect that service was completed is prima facie evidence of service. (d) A notice served under this section does not affect a lienholder's right to accelerate the maturity date of the debt in the future nor does it waive past defaults. (e) This section does not create an exclusive method for waiver and rescission of acceleration or affect the accrual of a cause of action and the running of the related limitations period under Section 16.035 (e) on any subsequent maturity date, accelerated or otherwise, of the note or obligation or series of notes or obligations. Added by Acts 2015, 84th Leg., R.S., Ch. 759 (H.B. 2067 ), Sec. 1, eff. June 17, 2015. SUBCHAPTER C. RESIDUAL LIMITATIONS PERIOD Sec. 16.051. RESIDUAL LIMITATIONS PERIOD. Every action for which there is no express limitations period, except an action for the recovery of real property, must be brought not later than four years after the day the cause of action accrues. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. SUBCHAPTER D. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS Sec. 16.061. RIGHTS NOT BARRED. (a) A right of action of this state or a political subdivision of the state, including a county, an incorporated city or town, a navigation district, a municipal utility district, a port authority, an entity acting under Chapter 54 , Transportation Code, a school district, or an entity created under Section 52 , Article III, or Section 59 , Article XVI, Texas Constitution, is not barred by any of the following sections: 16.001-16.004, 16.006 , 16.007 , 16.021-16.028, 16.030-16.032, 16.035-16.037, 16.051 , 16.062 , 16.063 , 16.065-16.067, 16.070 , 16.071 , 31.006 , or 71.021 . (b) In this section: (1) "Navigation district" means a navigation district organized under Section 52 , Article III, or Section 59 , Article XVI, Texas Constitution. (2) "Port authority" has the meaning assigned by Section 60.402 , Water Code. (3) "Municipal utility district" means a municipal utility district created under Section 52 , Article III, or Section 59 , Article XVI, Texas Constitution. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Amended by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.02, eff. Aug. 28, 1989; Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 782, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 30, 1993; Acts 1997, 75th Leg., ch. 1070, Sec. 47, eff. Sept. 1, 1997; Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1420, Sec. 8.204, eff. Sept. 1, 2001. Sec. 16.062. EFFECT OF DEATH. (a) The death of a person against whom or in whose favor there may be a cause of action suspends the running of an applicable statute of limitations for 12 months after the death. (b) If an executor or administrator of a decedent's estate qualifies before the expiration of the period provided by this section, the statute of limitations begins to run at the time of the qualification. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Sec. 16.063. TEMPORARY ABSENCE FROM STATE. The absence from this state of a person against whom a cause of action may be maintained suspends the running of the applicable statute of limitations for the period of the person's absence. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Sec. 16.064. EFFECT OF LACK OF JURISDICTION. (a) The period between the date of filing an action in a trial court and the date of a second filing of the same action in a different court suspends the running of the applicable statute of limitations for the period if: (1) because of lack of jurisdiction in the trial court where the action was first filed, the action is dismissed or the judgment is set aside or annulled in a direct proceeding; and (2) not later than the 60th day aft
perturbation.justification: The change from a 30-day to a 5-day notice period for indemnification claims likely contradicts Texas law. The Law Explanation suggests that, while parties can agree to shorten statutes of limitations, such changes must be reasonable. The Perturbation Explanation echoes this, stating a 5-day period might be commercially unreasonable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses statutes of limitations in general, and includes Sec. 16.070, it doesn't directly address the reasonableness standard. However, the LLM's Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, makes it clear that this change creates a potentially unenforceable term. The modification significantly weakens the indemnified party's ability to adequately prepare a response, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Establishment shall retain such records for five (5) years following the date of manufacture, or such longer period of time if consistent with Regulatory Standards, and shall make available to Apollo copies of such records; and upon the expiration of such period, Establishment shall contact Apollo and give Apollo the option to have such quality control documentation transferred to Apollo or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Establishment shall retain such records for two (2) years following the date of manufacture, or such longer period of time if consistent with Regulatory Standards, and shall make available to Apollo copies of such records; and upon the expiration of such period, Establishment shall contact Apollo and give Apollo the option to have such quality control documentation transferred to Apollo or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to two years may conflict with FDA requirements for medical device manufacturers, which often require longer retention periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 820.180(b) states that all records shall be retained for a period at least equivalent to the design and expected life of the device, but not less than two years from the date of release for commercial distribution by the manufacturer. While the changed text complies with the minimum, a complex medical device is likely to have an expected life longer than two years. Thus, a blanket two-year retention policy could violate the regulation if the product's life expectancy is greater than two years.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the record retention period from five years to two years. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 820.180(b) requires records to be retained for a period at least equivalent to the design and expected life of the device, but not less than two years. The Perturbation Explanation mentions that FDA requirements may require longer retention periods than two years. The reduction to two years, while meeting the minimum, may violate the regulation if the product's life expectancy is greater than two years, thus creating a contradiction because the blanket two-year policy is not consistent with potentially longer retention requirements based on device lifespan.
